point_001: The parameter count comparison is misleading: KALM base and large models have substantially more parameters than reported (e.g., ~550M and ~700M respectively) once entity embeddings are included, yet the paper makes claims about parameter efficiency relative to GPT-2 that do not account for these additional parameters.
point_002: The paper lacks sufficient ablation studies to understand the contribution of individual components, such as the effect of entity embeddings in the input alone, the impact of different n-gram matching cutoffs, and the sensitivity to the hyperparameter α balancing the two training objectives.
point_003: The motivation for using a margin loss for entity prediction rather than cross-entropy is not adequately justified, and no comparison between the two loss functions is provided.
point_004: The so-called 'zero-shot' QA experiments appear to be few-shot in practice, since the model is provided with several example question-answer pairs (dummy QAs) at inference time; the role and selection criteria of these dummy QAs are not clearly explained.
point_005: The paper does not demonstrate fine-tuning performance on diverse downstream knowledge-intensive tasks (e.g., KILT benchmarks, FEVER, Wizard-of-Wikipedia, extractive QA), making it unclear whether KALM's pretraining advantage transfers robustly beyond zero-shot and probing settings.
point_006: The comparison with closely related work such as Entities as Experts (EaE) is insufficient; EaE outperforms KALM on LAMA probing tasks, yet the paper claims fuzzy matching is superior to precise entity linking without adequately reconciling this contradiction.
point_007: The novelty of the approach is limited given prior work (e.g., ERNIE, EaE, CorefBERT, Dynamic Entity Representations) that also incorporates entity information into language model pretraining; the paper does not clearly articulate what distinguishes KALM from these methods or when it should be preferred.
point_008: Details of the dictionary lookup procedure are underspecified: it is unclear whether tokens are lowercased, how misspellings or out-of-vocabulary entities are handled, and what happens when an entity seen at inference time was not present during training.
point_009: The paper only explores one method of integrating entity information (summing word and entity embeddings); alternative integration strategies are not considered or discussed.
point_010: Experiments are limited to autoregressive (GPT-style) architectures; it is not shown whether the proposed pretraining technique generalizes to encoder-decoder models such as T5.
point_011: The LAMA evaluation metrics are not explained in the paper (abbreviations are not expanded), making it difficult for readers unfamiliar with the benchmark to interpret the results.
point_012: The paper does not investigate whether pretraining on more data (without entity information) could yield comparable gains, making it hard to isolate the benefit of entity knowledge versus additional training signal.
point_013: It is not explored whether entity-aware pretraining could be applied by fine-tuning an existing pretrained language model rather than training from scratch, which would be a more practical and computationally efficient approach.
